introMessage: You create. You deserve to be paid and protected. Let's work out what your rights are.

options.label: I haven't been paid for my work
The other party broke the terms we agreed
Someone used my work without permission
I wasn't credited for my work
The project was cancelled after I'd started
They kept changing the brief without extra pay
A platform removed or demonetised my content

questions.helpText: For payment disputes, use the date the payment was due. For copyright, use the date you first discovered the infringement.

questions.text: What type of creative work is this?
questions.helpText: This helps us apply the right legal framework.

questions.text: Was there a contract or written agreement?
questions.helpText: A written contract makes your position significantly stronger. Verbal agreements are still legally binding but harder to prove.

options.label: Yes, a written contract or email chain
Yes, but it was verbal only
Partial — some things were agreed in writing

questions.helpText: Claims under £10,000 can go through the Small Claims Court — it's cheap, fast, and designed for individuals.
A formal letter before action is required before you can go to court. We can help you draft one.

options.label: Yes, by formal letter or legal notice

questions.helpText: No response to a letter before action is itself evidence — it shows the court you tried to resolve things first.
Don't worry if you don't have much yet. We'll help you build your case.

options.label: Emails agreeing the brief and fee

strong.message: Based on what you've told us, you have strong grounds to pursue this. Here's how to take it forward.

maybe.message: You have a case worth pursuing. The key step is getting your evidence together and sending a proper letter before action.

difficult.message: Your position needs strengthening. Here's what to do to build your case before taking formal action.
